rationale_for_baseline_tier: A seborrhoeic dermatitis flare is benign and managed at home with topical antifungal, a short low-potency facial steroid and gentle skin care, with routine review to establish maintenance (T1). Facial cellulitis routes to GP/ED (T4); suspected lupus routes to GP/specialist.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: One area of the face becoming hot, deeply red, swollen and painful with a fever (spreading infection), or new joint pains with a sun-worsened cheek rash that spares the nose creases
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — GP/ED (cellulitis); prompt GP (lupus features)
escalation_edges.rationale: Facial cellulitis needs antibiotics; lupus features need assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If one part of your face becomes hot, deeply red, swollen and painful with a fever — see a doctor / go to ED that day. If you develop joint pains and a sun-worsened cheek rash, see your GP for tests.

contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Reassure about the benign, chronic nature; give a facial-cellulitis/lupus safety-net.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Use the antifungal cream and shampoo and a short course of the mild steroid cream, and switch to a gentle cleanser (no scrubbing). It should settle over a few days; keep the antifungal shampoo going now and then to prevent flares.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Self-care + GP review at ~2 weeks; GP/ED for facial cellulitis; GP for lupus features
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: GP/ED for a hot, deeply painful, spreading facial redness with fever.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Facial/scalp seborrhoeic dermatitis flare (involves nasolabial folds); lupus and facial cellulitis screened negative; ketoconazole + short hydrocortisone + gentle skin care; maintenance plan.

critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A facial cellulitis is mislabelled as seborrhoeic dermatitis and spreads.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Not distinguishing from lupus / no antifungal management.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: ED/ambulance for a benign seborrhoeic dermatitis flare — unnecessary.
